content.text: The earliest technology of printing was developed in East Asia, specifically in China, Japan, and Korea. This was a system of hand printing. From AD 594 onwards, books in China were printed by rubbing paper against the inked surface of woodblocks.

content.title: Accordion Book
content.text: Since both sides of the thin, porous sheet could not be printed, the traditional Chinese ‘accordion book’ was folded and stitched at the side. Skilled craftsmen duplicated the beauty of calligraphy with remarkable accuracy.

The imperial state in China was a major producer of printed material for a long time. The huge bureaucratic system recruited personnel through civil service examinations. Textbooks for these exams were printed in vast numbers under state sponsorship.

By the seventeenth century, urban culture bloomed in China, and the uses of print diversified. It was no longer used just by scholar-officials. Merchants used print for trade information, and reading became a leisure activity. Fictional narratives, poetry, and autobiographies became popular.

content.title: Shift to Mechanical Printing
content.text: In the late nineteenth century, Western printing techniques and mechanical presses were imported as Western powers established outposts in China. Shanghai became the hub of this new print culture.

Buddhist missionaries from China introduced hand-printing technology into Japan around AD 768-770. The oldest Japanese book, printed in AD 868, is the Buddhist Diamond Sutra, containing six sheets of text and woodcut illustrations.

In medieval Japan, poets and prose writers were regularly published, and books were cheap and abundant. Printing of visual material led to interesting practices. In the late eighteenth century, in Edo (now Tokyo), illustrated paintings depicted an elegant urban culture involving artists and courtesans.

content.title: Ukiyo Prints
procedure: Examine the works of Kitagawa Utamaro, born in Edo in 1753.
Identify the themes of 'ukiyo' (pictures of the floating world), which depicted ordinary human experiences.
Observe how these prints influenced European artists like Manet, Monet, and Van Gogh.
content.observation: Ukiyo prints captured urban life and ordinary experiences, eventually travelling to the US and Europe.
content.conclusion: Visual print material played a significant role in documenting and exporting cultural experiences.

topics.topic_name: The Journey of Paper and Print

content.text: For centuries, silk and spices flowed from China into Europe via the Silk Route. In the eleventh century, Chinese paper reached Europe through the same route, making the production of manuscripts by scribes possible.

content.title: Marco Polo's Contribution
content.text: In 1295, Marco Polo, a great explorer, returned to Italy from China. He brought back the knowledge of woodblock printing. Italians began producing books with woodblocks, and the technology soon spread across Europe.

While luxury editions were still handwritten on expensive vellum (animal skin), merchants and students in university towns preferred cheaper printed copies. As demand grew, booksellers began exporting books and holding book fairs.

Handwritten manuscripts had limitations: copying was expensive, laborious, and time-consuming. They were fragile, awkward to handle, and could not be carried easily. This created a need for quicker and cheaper reproduction.

Johann Gutenberg developed the first-known printing press in the 1430s at Strasbourg, Germany. Having grown up on an agricultural estate, he was familiar with wine and olive presses. He also had expertise as a goldsmith.

content.title: Innovation in Design
content.text: Gutenberg adapted the olive press model for the printing press and used lead moulds for casting metal types for the alphabet. By 1448, he perfected the system and printed the Bible as his first book.

The first printed books closely resembled handwritten manuscripts. Metal letters imitated ornamental styles, and borders were often hand-painted with foliage. Space was left for decoration so purchasers could choose their own designs.

content.title: Growth of Printing
items: 1450–1550: Printing presses were set up in most European countries.
Late 15th Century: 20 million copies of printed books in European markets.
16th Century: The number rose to about 200 million copies.
This shift from hand printing to mechanical printing is known as the Print Revolution.

notes.section_title: 3. The Print Revolution and Its Impact

content.text: Printing reduced the cost of books and reduced the time and labour required for production. This led to a flood of books in the market, creating a new culture of reading.

content.title: From Oral to Reading Culture
content.text: Earlier, reading was restricted to elites, while commoners lived in a world of oral culture (listening to ballads and folk tales). Printers bridged this gap by publishing illustrated popular ballads and folk tales that could be sung or recited in villages and taverns.
As a result, the boundary between oral and reading cultures blurred. Common people who could not read now heard printed material being read aloud, intermingling the hearing and reading publics.

topics.topic_name: Religious Debates and the Fear of Print

content.text: Print introduced a new world of debate and discussion. It allowed people to circulate ideas that disagreed with established authorities. However, many feared that uncontrolled printing would spread rebellious and 'irreligious' thoughts.

content.title: The Protestant Reformation
content.text: In 1517, Martin Luther wrote 'Ninety Five Theses' criticising Roman Catholic practices. Print allowed his ideas to spread rapidly, leading to the Protestant Reformation. Luther hailed printing as 'the ultimate gift of God'.

The Roman Catholic Church was troubled by these developments. In the sixteenth century, Menocchio, an Italian miller, reinterpreted the Bible after reading available books, formulating views that enraged the Church. This led the Church to impose controls and maintain an 'Index of Prohibited Books' from 1558.

topics.topic_name: Spread of Literacy and New Forms of Literature

content.text: Through the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, churches set up schools in villages, pushing literacy rates in some parts of Europe to 60-80%. This triggered a virtual reading mania.

content.title: Popular Forms of Reading
items: Chapbooks: Pocket-sized books sold by travelling pedlars (chapmen) for a penny in England.
Bibliotheque Bleue: Low-priced, blue-covered small books in France.
Periodical Press: Combined current affairs with entertainment (newspapers and journals).
Scientific and Philosophical Texts: Writings of Isaac Newton, Voltaire, and Rousseau became accessible to common people.

content.text: By the mid-eighteenth century, there was a conviction that books were a means of spreading progress and enlightenment. Louise-Sebastien Mercier declared the printing press the 'most powerful engine of progress'.

content.title: Print and the French Revolution
content.text: Print created conditions for the French Revolution by popularising Enlightenment ideas (reason over tradition), creating a culture of public debate, and mocking the royalty through cartoons and caricatures that highlighted social inequalities.

In the nineteenth century, primary education became compulsory, making children a critical category of readers. A children's press was set up in France in 1857, publishing edited versions of folk tales (like those collected by the Grimm Brothers in Germany).

Women became prominent as readers and writers. Penny magazines were meant for them, along with manuals on housekeeping. Many novels of the era (Jane Austen, Brontë sisters, George Eliot) defined a new type of woman with independent thought and strength.

content.title: Self-Education of Workers
content.text: Lending libraries became instruments for educating white-collar workers and artisans. As working hours were shortened, workers spent time on self-improvement, writing political tracts and autobiographies.

Mechanical improvements continuously transformed print. Richard M. Hoe perfected the power-driven cylindrical press, capable of printing 8,000 sheets per hour. Later, the offset press allowed printing in six colours.

Publishers developed new strategies to sustain business, especially during the Great Depression. They introduced cheap paperback editions and used dust covers (book jackets), a twentieth-century innovation.

notes.section_title: 6. India and the World of Print

topics.topic_name: Manuscripts Before the Age of Print

content.text: India had a rich tradition of handwritten manuscripts in Sanskrit, Arabic, Persian, and vernacular languages. These were written on palm leaves or handmade paper and preserved between wooden covers.

content.title: Limitations of Manuscripts
content.text: Manuscripts were expensive and fragile. They were not widely used in everyday life because scripts were often difficult to read. In pre-colonial Bengal, students often learnt to write from a teacher's dictation without actually reading texts.

topics.topic_name: The Arrival of Print in India

content.text: The printing press first arrived in Goa with Portuguese missionaries in the mid-sixteenth century. Jesuit priests printed several tracts in Konkani. By 1579, the first Tamil book was printed at Cochin.

The English language press was initiated by private enterprise. James Augustus Hickey began editing the 'Bengal Gazette' in 1780, a weekly magazine independent of colonial influence. Hickey published advertisements and gossip about Company officials, leading to persecution by Warren Hastings.

notes.section_title: 7. Religious Reform and Public Debates in India

content.text: The early nineteenth century was a time of intense religious and social debates. Printed tracts and newspapers spread new ideas and shaped the nature of these discussions on issues like sati, monotheism, and idolatry.

content.title: Key Publications
items: Rammohun Roy: Published 'Sambad Kaumudi' (from 1821) for social reform.
Hindu Orthodoxy: Published 'Samachar Chandrika' to oppose Roy's views.
Muslim Press: Used lithographic presses for holy scriptures and fatwas (Deoband Seminary) to counter colonial influence.

content.title: Vernacular Connectivity
content.text: Print encouraged the reading of religious texts in vernacular languages. Cheap lithographic editions of the 'Ramcharitmanas' flooded markets. Newspapers connected people in different parts of India, creating pan-Indian identities.

notes.section_title: 8. New Forms of Publication in India

content.text: The novel, an ideally European form, soon acquired Indian forms and styles, reflecting local experiences. Other literary forms like lyrics, short stories, and essays on social-political matters became popular.

content.title: Raja Ravi Varma
content.text: By the end of the nineteenth century, printers like Raja Ravi Varma produced images for mass circulation. Cheap prints and calendars allowed even the poor to decorate their homes with iconic images.
Caricatures and cartoons began appearing in journals by the 1870s, commenting on social changes, Western tastes, and mocking both colonial and nationalist figures.

Middle-class reading increased as liberal husbands and fathers began educating women at home or in schools. However, conservative households feared that education would lead to widows (Hindus) or corruption (Muslims).

content.title: Pioneering Women Writers
items: Rashsundari Debi: Secretly learnt to read and wrote 'Amar Jiban' (1876), the first full-length Bengali autobiography.
Kailashbashini Debi: Wrote about the hardships of women imprisoned at home.
Tarabai Shinde and Pandita Ramabai: Wrote about the miserable lives of upper-caste Hindu women.
Begum Rokeya Sakhawat Hossein: Condemned men for withholding education from women.

content.text: Cheap small books were sold at crossroads in Madras, allowing poor people to buy them. Public libraries were set up, often as a mark of prestige for rich patrons. Issues of caste discrimination became a central theme in print.

content.title: Caste and Class Voices
content.text: Jyotiba Phule wrote about the injustices of the caste system in 'Gulamgiri' (1871). Millworkers in Kanpur and Bombay also wrote about their experiences; Kashibaba published 'Chhote Aur Bade Ka Sawal' to show links between caste and class exploitation.

Initially, the East India Company's censorship was directed at Englishmen critical of the Company. By the 1820s, regulations were passed to control press freedom. However, in 1835, Thomas Macaulay formulated rules that restored some freedoms.

content.title: Vernacular Press Act (1878)
content.text: After the 1857 revolt, the government sought to clamp down on the nationalist 'native' press. Modelled on Irish Press Laws, the Vernacular Press Act gave the government extensive rights to censor provincial newspapers. Seditious reports led to warnings and confiscation of machinery.

Despite this, nationalist newspapers grew. Balgangadhar Tilak's support for Punjab revolutionaries in his 'Kesari' led to his imprisonment, sparking widespread protests. During the World Wars, further controls like the Defence of India Act suppressed news about movements like Quit India.

glossary.definition: The art of beautiful and stylised writing.
A parchment made from the skin of animals, used for writing before paper became common.
In letterpress printing, a board which is pressed onto the back of the paper to get the impression from the type.
The person who composes the text for printing.
A metal frame in which types are laid and the text composed.
A historical account or folk tale in verse, usually sung or recited.
Places where people gathered to drink alcohol, eat, and exchange news.
A sixteenth-century movement to reform the Catholic Church, led by figures like Martin Luther.
A former Roman Catholic court for identifying and punishing heretics.
Beliefs which do not follow the accepted teachings of the Church.
A system of governance in which absolute power is exercised by an individual, unregulated by legal checks.
An annual publication giving astronomical data and information important for everyday life.
Pocket-size books sold by travelling pedlars (chapmen) in England.
Legal scholars of Islam and the sharia.
A legal pronouncement on Islamic law given by a mufti.
Action, speech, or writing seen as opposing the government.

summary_points: Printing began in East Asia (China, Japan, Korea) as woodblock printing before moving to mechanical systems.
Marco Polo brought woodblock printing methodology from China to Europe in 1295, leading to its widespread adoption.
Johann Gutenberg's invention of the mechanical printing press in the 1430s triggered a revolution in book production and literacy.
The spread of print played a crucial role in religious movements like the Protestant Reformation and political events like the French Revolution.
The nineteenth century saw innovations like the cylindrical press and the rise of new reader categories: children, women, and workers.
India had a rich manuscript tradition, but the arrival of the printing press in the sixteenth century transformed social and religious debates.
Print culture in India allowed reformers to challenge traditions (e.g., Rammohun Roy) and enabled women and lower castes to share their experiences.
The colonial government used censorship, notably the Vernacular Press Act of 1878, to suppress nationalist sentiments expressed through print.
Despite repression, print became a powerful vehicle for the nationalist movement and the cultivation of public opinion in India.
